sourceTitle: Connect projects and docs to get more done, faster

questions.question: Where do you go to add the ready‑made Projects & Tasks system to your workspace?

options.text: The **Templates** menu in the sidebar
options.explanation: The guide says you duplicate the Projects & Tasks template from the Templates menu.

options.text: The **Settings** → **Integrations** page
options.explanation: Integrations are for connecting external apps, not for adding Notion templates.

options.text: The **Help Center** search bar
options.explanation: Searching the Help Center only shows documentation; it doesn’t add a template.

options.text: The **Import** → **CSV** dialog
options.explanation: CSV import brings in data, not a pre‑built template.

questions.question: Which property lets you indicate that a project must be finished before another one can start?

options.text: Blocking
options.explanation: The Blocking property is specifically mentioned for marking prerequisite projects.

options.text: Dependencies
options.explanation: Dependencies are used on the Tasks database to show task‑to‑task links, not project prerequisites.

options.text: Priority
options.explanation: Priority ranks importance but does not enforce sequencing.

options.text: Estimate
options.explanation: Estimate measures effort, not ordering.

questions.question: What does a **Relation** property create between two databases?

options.text: A two‑way connection that lets you link records in both databases
options.explanation: Relation properties are two‑way, enabling each side to see the linked record.

options.text: A one‑way copy of data from one database to another
options.explanation: Relations are not copies; they are links that work both ways.

options.text: An automatic calculation of totals across databases
options.explanation: Calculations are performed by Rollup properties, not Relation.

options.text: A visual timeline that merges the two databases
options.explanation: Timelines are a view type; they don’t create the underlying link.

questions.question: If you want a task that repeats every Monday to appear automatically in a database, which feature should you use?

options.text: A repeating template
options.explanation: Repeating templates can be set to generate a new entry on a daily, weekly, or monthly schedule.

options.text: A button with a scheduled trigger
options.explanation: Buttons run when clicked; they don’t schedule automatic creation.

options.text: A filtered view that shows only Monday tasks
options.explanation: Filters only display existing items; they don’t create new ones.

options.text: An AI autofill property set to ‘Weekly Summary’
options.explanation: AI autofill fills text properties, not schedule entry creation.

questions.question: How can you use a button to streamline a bug‑reporting workflow?

options.text: Configure the button to add a new entry to the bug‑tracking database and set properties like “Date reported” and “Reported by” automatically
options.explanation: The guide describes using a button to create a bug entry and pre‑populate key properties.

options.text: Create a button that opens a new page where users manually type the bug details
options.explanation: That still requires manual entry; the benefit is automation.

options.text: Use a button to export the bug database to CSV
options.explanation: Exporting doesn’t simplify reporting; it moves data out of Notion.

options.text: Add a button that deletes all open bugs after they are resolved
options.explanation: Deletion isn’t part of the described streamlined reporting flow.

questions.question: Which view type would let you see tasks that block other tasks as visual arrows?

options.text: Timeline view (with the Dependencies property visible)
options.explanation: The Dependencies property appears only in timeline view and shows blocking relationships.

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view shows status columns but not dependency arrows.

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Table view lists rows; it doesn’t display visual dependency lines.

options.text: Gallery view
options.explanation: Gallery view focuses on card previews, not task dependencies.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using the **AI autofill** property with the “Summary” option on a project page?

options.text: It continuously generates an up‑to‑date summary of the page content, keeping teammates informed without manual edits
options.explanation: The Summary option auto‑updates as the page changes, providing a real‑time overview.

options.text: It automatically creates a new project database entry for every related task
options.explanation: Autofill fills text properties; it does not create database entries.

options.text: It replaces the need for a separate “Description” property
options.explanation: While it adds a summary, a dedicated description can still be useful for static details.

options.text: It syncs the project page with an external AI writing tool
options.explanation: The feature works entirely within Notion; no external sync is required.

questions.question: When customizing the Projects database, which property would be most useful for distinguishing the person who leads a project from those who contribute?

options.text: Collaborators
options.explanation: The guide suggests using the Collaborators property to tag contributors separately from the owner.

options.text: Blocking
options.explanation: Blocking marks prerequisite projects, not people roles.

options.text: Estimate
options.explanation: Estimate measures effort, not ownership.

options.text: Priority
options.explanation: Priority ranks urgency, not team roles.

questions.question: Why might a team choose to import their Asana tasks into Notion rather than keep using both tools side‑by‑side?

options.text: To achieve a single source of truth, reduce context‑switching, and give every team member full transparency within one workspace
options.explanation: The article emphasizes that using multiple tools fragments workflow; importing consolidates information.

options.text: Because Notion can automatically sync changes back to Asana in real time
options.explanation: Notion’s importer is a one‑time migration; it does not provide continuous sync.

options.text: To replace Asana’s timeline view with Notion’s board view
options.explanation: While Notion offers multiple views, the primary reason is consolidation, not view replacement.

options.text: Because Asana does not support task dependencies, which Notion adds automatically
options.explanation: Asana does support dependencies; the advantage lies in unifying data, not feature gaps.

questions.question: Which combination of features would best let a manager automatically assign all overdue tasks to the person who clicks a button, and also set the task status to “In Progress”?

options.text: Create a button that runs two actions: (1) update the “Assignee” property to the current user for all overdue tasks, and (2) set the “Status” property to “In Progress”
options.explanation: Buttons can execute multiple actions in one click, exactly as described in the guide.

options.text: Add an AI autofill property that marks overdue tasks as “In Progress”
options.explanation: AI autofill fills text, not bulk property updates across multiple records.

options.text: Use a repeating template that creates a new task every day titled “Overdue Review”
options.explanation: Repeating templates generate new entries but don’t reassign existing overdue tasks.

options.text: Set up a Relation between the Tasks and a “User” database and rely on a filtered view to show overdue tasks
options.explanation: Relations and filtered views display data but don’t automate reassignment.
